channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Pardons Former Congressman Convicted of Insider Trading
item.description: Stephen Buyer, a former Republican representative from Indiana, was convicted of trading stock related to two deals before they were made public.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:description: Stephen Buyer arriving for his insider trading trial in New York City in 2023.

item.title: Trump Greets Farmers in Wisconsin, but Says He Could Be Home Watching TV
item.description: President Trump was in Wisconsin to reassure farmers who have been stung by his tariff policies and rising fuel prices from the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.media:description: President Trump traveled to western Wisconsin, which is home to large beef, dairy and crop farms, to shore up support for a Republican loyalist running for re-election in a purple district.

item.title: Amid Mounting Democratic Concern, Platner Says His Past Is Being ‘Weaponized’
item.description: The presumptive Democratic Senate nominee in Maine said the state would have his back in the face of accusations he has denied.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk and Katie Glueck
item.media:description: Graham Platner, a Democratic candidate for U.S. Senate, held a campaign event on Friday at a theater in Bar Harbor, Maine, where he told attendees that his state would have his back in the face of “politically motivated” attacks.

item.title: Trump Says Bill Pulte, His New Intelligence Director, Should Slash Staff
item.description: The president suggested that employees who worked for previous Democratic presidents were among those who should be fired.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:description: Bill Pulte.

item.title: Park Ranger Dies After Falling Into a Crevasse on Mt. McKinley
item.description: Robin Pendery, 33, who was part of a climbing patrol, was near a camp at about 14,000 feet on the mountain in Alaska when she fell, the National Park Service said.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:description: Mount McKinley in Alaska is the tallest peak in North America.

item.title: Xavier Becerra Advances in California Governor Race
item.description: Mr. Becerra was long dismissed in the contest until the abrupt departure of Eric Swalwell created a surprise path for an experienced Democrat.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:description: Xavier Becerra at a phone banking event in San Jose, Calif., on Saturday.

item.title: Iran Fired Drones Toward Strait of Hormuz With U.S. Shooting Down at Least 4, U.S. Military Says
item.description: The latest violence between the United States and Iran threatened a cease-fire and risked further stoking tensions in the region.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Vessels anchored in the Strait of Hormuz as seen from Musandam, Oman, on Tuesday.

item.title: Patel Said to Have Fired Five F.B.I. Analysts Over Memo on Catholic Extremists
item.description: It is the administration’s latest effort to force out law enforcement personnel it accuses of “weaponizing” the government against President Trump and his supporters.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Kash Patel, the F.B.I. director, speaking at a hearing this year on Capitol Hill. Some former federal law enforcement officials have said law enforcement agencies are being turned into ideologically driven arms of the presidency.

item.title: Ebola Cases May Top 20,000 in Three Months, C.D.C. Says
item.description: Still, the risk to people in the United States remains low, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention said in a report Friday.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health workers in the isolation ward of a newly established Ebola treatment center in Ituri Province in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, the epicenter of the Ebola outbreak, last month.

item.title: Justice Dept. Promises to Drop Trump’s $1.8 Billion Fund
item.description: It was the department’s clearest statement to date that it was pulling back from a plan to use taxpayer money to make payments to people who claimed to have been politically persecuted.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Twin filings on Friday are the strongest signal yet that the Justice Department is serious about dropping the idea of the fund.

item.title: Amid Graham Platner Controversy, Where Does the Maine Senate Race Go Next?
item.description: New revelations about Graham Platner have left Democrats anxious.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina
item.media:description: Graham Platner

item.title: Pentagon Cuts 180 Religious Identities From Military Personnel Records
item.description: The new policy, which the Pentagon framed as a largely administrative action, leaves just 31 religious categories to choose from, 22 of which are Christian.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay, Alexandra E. Petri and Aimee Ortiz
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Mormons (Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints)
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: In a video posted to social media on March 24, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said that the military’s chaplain corps had been “infected by political correctness and secular humanism” under previous administrations.

item.title: Trump Arch Will Need Red Lights, F.A.A. Says
item.description: The Federal Aviation Administration advised that if the Trump administration adds red obstruction lights to the arch design, it is likely to pose no risk to local aviation.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:description: The F.A.A. says the proposed 259-foot tall arch to be built on Memorial Circle in Washington would likely not pose aviation safety risks.

item.title: First U.S. Screwworm Case Since 1960s Prompts Texas Emergency Response
item.description: The infections are the first detected in the United States since the 1960s. Officials are aggressively trying to prevent the parasite from spreading in the nation’s largest cattle-producing state.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval
item.media:description: Officials are dispersing millions of sterile screwworm flies in the vicinity of where the infected calf was found.

item.title: Graham Platner Controversy Deepens Democratic Unease Before Maine Senate Primary
item.description: The party’s politicians, officials and strategists wrestled with how to respond to new reporting about Graham Platner, with worries rising about his vulnerabilities in a general election.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:description: Graham Platner has been widely expected to win the Democratic primary election for Senate on Tuesday in Maine.

item.title: Man Who Schemed With Au Pair to Kill His Wife Is Sentenced to Life
item.description: Brendan Banfield was found guilty of murdering his wife and another man, who was lured in through a fetish website. The plan involved his lover, who is now serving 10 years in prison.
item.dc:creator: Jacey Fortin and JoAnna Daemmrich
item.media:description: Brendan Banfield arriving to testify in his double murder trial in January in Fairfax, Va.

item.title: Judge Says Trump Officials Must Restart Asylum and Immigration Processing
item.description: The judge invalidated policies the Trump administration enacted last year that halted asylum grants, as well as the processing of immigration benefits for people from 39 countries.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Immigrants waiting for their appointments at the Annandale Immigration Court in Annandale, Va., last month.

item.title: Chuck Schumer Privately Backs Haley Stevens in Michigan Democratic Senate Primary
item.description: Chuck Schumer, the Democratic minority leader, has remained officially neutral on the race, but in conversations with donors, he has been clear about which candidate he supports.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:description: Representative Haley Stevens of Michigan, a moderate Democrat, is locked in a competitive three-way primary race for Senate.

item.title: Casting Her 10,000th Vote in a Row, Collins Sets a Senate Record
item.description: The Maine Republican, who is in the middle of a tumultuous re-election race, became the first senator in history to reach the threshold without missing a vote.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:description: Senator Susan Collins of Maine has not missed a vote since taking office in 1997, a feat that colleagues on both sides of the aisle recognized as impressive.

item.title: Spelman College Names Ayanna Howard, an A.I. Expert, as President
item.description: The appointment of Ayanna Howard, a roboticist, was a departure for the top-ranked H.B.C.U., which has a liberal arts focus.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: Nydia Blas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Spelman College, the historically Black liberal arts school for women, named Ayanna Howard as its new president on Friday.

item.title: Trump Keeps Immunity from I.R.S. Audits, Even as $1.8 Billion Fund Falls Away
item.description: Even as they rebelled against a $1.8 billion fund for President Trump’s allies, Republicans looked the other way as his administration granted him potentially lucrative tax protections.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:description: President Trump has long sought to avoid scrutiny of his taxes, and during his first run for office refused to release his tax returns, breaking a norm for presidential candidates.

item.title: Deal to Renew FISA Surveillance Law Falters in the Senate Over Pulte
item.description: Democrats who had been expected to supply the votes necessary to advance it balked after President Trump named Bill Pulte to head the intelligence apparatus.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.media:description: Mr. Trump tapped Mr. Pulte, a businessman and close political ally who has used his role as federal housing director as a perch to carry out a campaign of retribution against the president’s perceived enemies.

item.title: James Handy, Actor in ‘Top Gun: Maverick,’ Is Found Stabbed to Death
item.description: The 81-year-old had parts in films including “Top Gun: Maverick” and “Arachnophobia.” The son of his girlfriend was arrested and charged with murder.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango and Rebecca Fairley Raney
item.media:credit: ABC Photo Archives/Disney General Entertainment Content, via Getty Images
item.media:description: James Handy’s many roles in movies and television included a bartender in “Top Gun: Maverick” and the director of the C.I.A. in “Alias,” as well as a borough commander in this episode of “NYPD Blue.”

item.title: They Shut the Golden Gate Bridge for 4 Hours. Now They Face Up to 15 Years in Prison.
item.description: Activists blocked the iconic span to protest the spending of American tax dollars on Israeli military efforts in Gaza. San Francisco’s prosecutor is now seeking a strict punishment.
item.dc:creator: Heather Knight
item.media:credit: Lea Suzuki/San Francisco Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Pedestrians and bicyclists waited outside the pedestrian gate on the south side of the Golden Gate Bridge when the bridge was closed because of protesters in 2024.

item.title: Appeals Court Appears Skeptical of Trump’s Ballroom Arguments
item.description: A judge asked what speedy legal recourse people might have if the government decided to bulldoze the Statue of Liberty. A government lawyer said he thought there was none.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:description: In May, President Trump brought reporters to the site of his ballroom project, where construction has continued despite a pending lawsuit.

item.title: Many Trump Voters Are Unhappy With Handling of Iran, Economy and More Issues
item.description: The president’s base is thought to be unmovable. But one-third of these voters say they are unhappy about certain issues and are not knee-jerk loyalists.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Igielnik
item.media:description: These voters see themselves as having few options.
